source_metadata.title_raw: Kenneth R. Hetge - A-19-047 - April 17,2019 - Tehachapi

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 17, 2019

Kenneth R. Hetge
City Councilmember, District 2
City of Tehachapi
333 S. Dennison Road
Tehachapi, CA 93561

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-19-047

Dear Mr. Hetge:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest.

        Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

        Regarding our advice under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kern County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

        Given that you are a Tehachapi City Councilmember disqualified under Section 1090 from taking part in the contracting process for repairs to the Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("the Airport"), may you still speak at a City Council meeting as a member of the public regarding your personal interest as an affected business owner? Likewise, under Section 1090, may you express your opinion to reporters and media outlets through public speech, such as by writing a newspaper article or appearing on a radio program?

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-047
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Unlike the Act, which includes limited exceptions that allow an otherwise disqualified public official from speaking as a member of the general public and speaking to the media, Section 1090 contains no similar exception. Accordingly, you may not attempt to influence the other members of the Tehachapi City Council at a meeting of the council even if you step down from the dais and speak in the same manner as other members of the public.

In regard to communications to reporters or the media, absent controlling authority, we can only conclude that you are not generally prohibited from public communications not intended to influence the execution of the contract by the City Council. However, you may wish to seek additional advice from the Attorney General's office if you make public communications intended to influence the execution of the contract.

FACTS

In the recent Schroeter Advice Letter, no. A-19-006, we determined that a Tehachapi City Councilmember who operates a business out of the Airport has a disqualifying financial interest, under Section 1090, in potential contracts for repairs to the airport. In the short-run, the repairs would potentially have a negative effect on business operations as it would affect access to the business. In the long-run, the repairs could positively impact the business by allowing planes to continue to safely fly in and out of the Airport. Ultimately, we determined that although the interested City Councilmember was disqualified from taking part in the contracting process under Section 1090, the "rule of necessity" permitted the City Council to contract for the necessary repairs to ensure that the Airport continued to operate safely. In light of the Schroeter Advice Letter, No. A-19-006, you have sought additional advice regarding actions you can take as a public official disqualified under Section 1090.

You are a member of the Tehachapi City Council. You and your wife own an aviation business on the Tehachapi Municipal Airport. You own and rent hangars, aircrafts, and provide aviation services. You are concerned that decisions being made by the City of Tehachapi regarding infrastructure and policies associated with the airport will impact your business, to the point that you may go out of business.

ANALYSIS

Separate from Section 1090, the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." (Section 87100.) Regulation 18704 defines "making," "participating in making," and "using official position to influence a decision." That regulation also provides limited exceptions to those definitions. Under Regulation 18704(d)(2), an otherwise disqualified public official may appear before his or her agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function as a member of the general public on matters related solely to his personal interests, including a business entity owned entirely by him or her, his or her immediate family, or some combination thereof. Under Regulation 18704(d)(4), an otherwise disqualified public official is not prohibited from making communications to the general public or media, such as writing a newspaper article or

File No. A-19-047
Page No. 3

appearing on a radio program and expressing his or her opinion regarding the matter in which he or
she has a disqualifying interest.

Unlike Regulation 18704, which pertains to the Act, Section 1090 does not expressly
include any similar exceptions to its disqualification of an interested public official. Instead, Section
1090 broadly provides that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district,
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Nor is any similar
exception found under related statutes.

Generally, Section 1090 precludes any direct or indirect attempt to influence the execution
of a contract to promote an official's personal interest. For example, Section 1091 permits a
governmental body to form a contract despite a public official's "remote" interest in the contract, so
long as the official properly recuses himself from the contracting process. (See Section 1091.)
However, Section 1091's remote interest exception "is not applicable to any officer interested in a
contract who influences or attempts to influence another member of the body or board of which he
or she is a member to enter [or refrain from entering] into the contract." (Section 1091(c).)

Moreover, the courts have established a broad standard for an official's involvement or
participation in the making of a contract under Section 1090:

"The decisional law, therefore, has not interpreted section 1090 in a hyper
technical manner, but holds that an official (or a public employee) may be convicted
of violation no matter whether he actually participated personally in the execution of
the questioned contract, if it is established that he had the opportunity to, and did,
influence execution directly or indirectly to promote his personal interest."

(People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.)

In this case, your interest as a private business owner is not "remote," under Section 1091
(nor does it qualify as a "noninterest" under Section 1091.5). Rather, you are more than remotely
interested in any governmental contract for repairs to the Tehachapi Municipal Airport, given that
the repairs and the repair process may have significant financial effects on your business. Thus,
under Section 1090 you may not directly or indirectly attempt to influence the City Council in the
execution of the contract.

In regard to your specific questions, any attempt to influence the City Council is not permitted and
we have not located any authority, including statutes and case law, permitting a disqualified public
official with a non-remote interest to influence other members of the governing body through
comments made directly to the City Council at a City Council meeting even if the comments are
made off of the dais and in the same manner as other members of the public. Moreover, for
purposes of communications to reporters or the media, such as by writing a newspaper article or
appearing on a radio program, public communications not intended to influence the execution of the
contract by the City Council are not generally prohibited. However, as you are prohibited from
making any attempt to influence the execution of the contract by the City Council, you may wish to
seek additional advice from the Attorney General's office if that is your intention. Without authority

File No. A-19-047
Page No. 4

to do so, the Commission is unable to establish a general exception to Section 1090 for
communications to the press.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[Signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

sections.question:
Given that you are a Tehachapi City Councilmember disqualified under Section 1090 from 
taking part in the contracting process for repairs to the Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("the Airport"), 
may you still speak at a City Council meeting as a member of the public regarding your personal 
interest as an affected business owner? Likewise, under Section 1090, may you express your 
opinion to reporters and media outlets through public speech, such as by writing a newspaper article 
or appearing on a radio program?
